agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"DynaPricing is a real-time dynamic delivery pricing system that adjusts prices based on demand signals. The CEO describes it at a high level with emphasis on automation, competitiveness, profitability, and guardrails. The spec is extremely vague — most critical business parameters are undefined.","bounded_context":"Pricing","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"DynaPricing","definition":"The dynamic pricing product/system that adjusts delivery prices in real time based on demand"},{"term":"Demand","definition":"A measure of delivery request volume relative to capacity — exact metric undefined (could be orders/hour, zone saturation, courier availability ratio, etc.)"},{"term":"Guardrails","definition":"Upper and/or lower bounds on price adjustments to prevent extreme pricing — thresholds undefined"},{"term":"Real-time pricing","definition":"Prices are computed and applied dynamically as demand conditions change, not on a fixed schedule"},{"term":"Base price","definition":"Implied but never stated — the reference price from which adjustments are made"},{"term":"Price adjustment","definition":"The delta applied to a delivery's price based on current demand conditions"}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Delivery prices must increase when demand is high relative to a baseline","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a business policy defining pricing strategy. A VP could decide to hold prices flat during high demand for a promotion or strategic reason. The direction of adjustment is a policy choice, not a system integrity constraint.","testable_assertion":"Given demand exceeds the high-demand threshold, the computed price must be greater than the base price for the same delivery parameters"},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Delivery prices must decrease when demand is low relative to a baseline","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a business policy. Management could choose to keep prices at baseline during low demand rather than discounting. It is a competitive strategy lever, not a data integrity constraint.","testable_assertion":"Given demand falls below the low-demand threshold, the computed price must be less than the base price for the same delivery parameters"},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Price adjustments must be computed and applied in real time — not batched on a fixed schedule","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Real-time vs batch is an operational policy. The business could decide hourly recalculation is sufficient. No data corruption occurs if pricing updates are delayed.","testable_assertion":"Given a change in demand conditions, the pricing system must reflect the new price within a defined latency SLA (SLA value undefined)"},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"The pricing system must be fully automated with no manual intervention required for routine price adjustments","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Automation level is an operational policy. The business could introduce manual approval steps for certain thresholds. No system corruption if a human is in the loop.","testable_assertion":"Given a demand change event, the system must compute and publish a new price without requiring human approval"},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Price adjustments must be bounded by guardrails — prices must not exceed a maximum ceiling or fall below a minimum floor","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a state integrity constraint. A price outside guardrail bounds represents a corrupt system state. Without enforced bounds, the system could charge $0 or infinite amounts, violating financial integrity, potentially regulatory/consumer-protection law, and causing irreversible business damage (refunds, legal exposure, loss of courier supply).","testable_assertion":"For any computed price P: P >= PRICE_FLOOR and P <= PRICE_CEILING, where PRICE_FLOOR > 0 and PRICE_CEILING is a defined maximum"},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"A delivery price must always be greater than zero","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"A zero or negative delivery price is a physical and financial impossibility — delivery has real cost. Allowing it would corrupt revenue accounting and is never a valid business state regardless of demand conditions.","testable_assertion":"For any computed delivery price P: P > 0"},{"id":"BR-7","rule":"The pricing system must balance competitiveness and profitability — prices must not consistently fall below cost","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Profitability targets and competitive positioning are strategic business policies that change with market conditions. A VP could authorize below-cost pricing to enter a new market. However, prolonged below-cost pricing without authorization would be a business concern.","testable_assertion":"Given a computed price P and the cost-to-deliver C for a delivery, the system should flag or prevent P < C unless an override policy is active"},{"id":"BR-8","rule":"Demand must be measured and quantifiable as an input to the pricing algorithm","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"If the system cannot determine demand, it cannot compute a price adjustment. A pricing decision made without a demand signal is not a dynamic price — it is arbitrary. The system is in a corrupt state if it produces a 'demand-based' price without actual demand data.","testable_assertion":"Every price adjustment event must reference a valid, timestamped demand measurement; a price must not be published if demand data is unavailable or stale beyond a defined threshold"}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Demand signal becomes unavailable or stale (e.g., data pipeline failure)","expected_behavior":"System must fall back to base price or last-known-good price rather than computing with missing data. Must not produce a 'dynamic' price without a demand signal."},{"scenario":"Demand oscillates rapidly around the high/low thresholds","expected_behavior":"Pricing must not flicker between high and low prices within seconds — some dampening or hysteresis mechanism is needed to prevent customer-visible price instability."},{"scenario":"Simultaneous extreme demand in multiple zones","expected_behavior":"Guardrails must apply per-delivery independently. System-wide load must not cause guardrails to be bypassed due to race conditions or resource exhaustion."},{"scenario":"Price floor and price ceiling converge or overlap due to misconfiguration","expected_behavior":"System must reject guardrail configurations where floor >= ceiling. This is a configuration integrity check."},{"scenario":"Customer sees one price at quote time but demand changes before confirmation","expected_behavior":"Business must decide: honor quoted price or re-price at confirmation? This is undefined in the spec and is a critical UX and revenue question."},{"scenario":"Demand is artificially inflated (e.g., bot attacks, order-cancel cycles)","expected_behavior":"Demand measurement should be resistant to manipulation. Artificial inflation could trigger unjustified surge pricing, damaging customer trust."},{"scenario":"Courier supply drops to zero in a zone","expected_behavior":"Undefined — is infinite demand met with max ceiling price, or should the system stop accepting orders? Pricing alone cannot solve a supply problem."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Dynamic pricing directly affects revenue per delivery and total order volume. Upside: capture surplus during peaks, stimulate demand during troughs. Downside: incorrect guardrails could hemorrhage margin (floor too low) or kill demand (ceiling too high). Price-below-cost scenarios without controls are an existential risk.","operational":"Fully automated pricing removes manual intervention but introduces operational complexity: monitoring demand signals, tuning algorithms, managing guardrail configurations, handling failover. Requires new observability and alerting infrastructure.","user":"Customers will experience variable pricing — this is a significant UX change. Perception of fairness is critical. Surge pricing without transparency (e.g., Uber backlash) damages brand trust. Couriers may game the system by declining deliveries in low-demand periods to trigger surge pricing.","risk":"HIGH. Regulatory risk in jurisdictions with price-gouging laws (especially during emergencies/natural disasters). Reputational risk from perceived unfair pricing. Technical risk from real-time system failures causing price anomalies. Financial risk from guardrail misconfiguration.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers (senders)","impact":"Directly affected — will pay variable prices. May perceive unfairness during high-demand surges. Need transparency into why prices change."},{"group":"Couriers/Drivers","impact":"Indirectly affected — higher prices during peak demand could increase earnings, but may also create perverse incentives to manipulate demand signals."},{"group":"Finance/Revenue team","impact":"Must model revenue impact, set guardrail thresholds, and monitor margin. Need reporting on pricing distribution and profitability."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Will receive complaints about price variability. Need tooling to explain pricing and potentially issue credits."},{"group":"Legal/Compliance","impact":"Must review for price-gouging regulations, consumer protection laws, and disclosure requirements in each operating jurisdiction."},{"group":"Product/UX","impact":"Must design transparent price communication. Customers need to understand why prices differ and when cheaper options are available."},{"group":"Operations/Dispatch","impact":"Pricing affects order volume patterns. Must ensure dispatch systems can handle demand shifts caused by price changes."}]},"confidence":{"level":30,"reasoning":"The CEO's description is an informal all-hands soundbite, not a product spec. Almost every critical parameter is undefined: demand metric, threshold values, guardrail bounds, base price calculation, latency SLA, price-lock policy, failover behavior, zone granularity, and regulatory constraints. The word 'probably' before guardrails introduces doubt about whether guardrails are even confirmed. This is a vision statement, not a specification. Confidence is very low because the spec is far too vague to build against.","high_confidence_areas":["Prices go up with high demand and down with low demand — directional intent is clear","System is automated, not manual","Some form of guardrails is intended","The goal is balancing competitiveness with profitability"],"low_confidence_areas":["How demand is measured (metric, granularity, source)","What the guardrail thresholds are (min/max prices, percentage caps)","Whether a base price exists and how it is calculated","Real-time latency requirements (milliseconds? seconds? minutes?)","Price-lock policy (is quoted price honored?)","Geographic/zone granularity of demand signals","Regulatory compliance requirements","Failover behavior when demand data is unavailable","Whether courier earnings are affected by dynamic pricing","Whether discounting below cost is permitted"],"assumptions":["A base price exists from which adjustments are computed","Guardrails include both a floor and a ceiling","Demand is measured per geographic zone, not globally","The system must remain available even when demand data is degraded","Price must always be positive","Real-time means sub-minute latency, not real-time in the strict embedded-systems sense"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Demand' is undefined. It could mean: orders per hour, orders relative to courier supply, zone saturation percentage, or predictive demand. The choice fundamentally changes the pricing algorithm and data infrastructure required.","affected_stakeholders":["Engineering","Finance","Product"],"options":["A) Orders per time window (simple volume)","B) Order-to-courier ratio (supply-demand balance)","C) Zone capacity utilization percentage","D) ML-predicted demand curve"],"recommendation":"B — order-to-courier ratio — because it captures both demand AND supply, which is what actually drives delivery feasibility and pricing pressure in logistics."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Guardrails' are described with 'probably' — it is unclear if they are confirmed, what form they take (absolute price bounds, percentage caps on adjustment, rate-of-change limits), or who sets them.","affected_stakeholders":["Finance","Legal","Product","Customers"],"options":["A) Hard absolute price floor and ceiling per zone","B) Percentage cap on adjustment from base price (e.g., max +200%)","C) Both absolute bounds AND rate-of-change limits","D) Soft guardrails with alerts but no hard enforcement"],"recommendation":"C — both absolute bounds and rate-of-change limits. Absolute bounds prevent extreme states; rate limits prevent jarring price swings. This is standard in logistics dynamic pricing."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"Price-lock policy is entirely undefined. When a customer is quoted a price and demand changes before order confirmation, it is unknown whether the quoted price is honored.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Product","Finance","Customer Support"],"options":["A) Lock price for a fixed window (e.g., 5 minutes) after quote","B) Re-price at confirmation time with no lock","C) Lock price but show expiration countdown"],"recommendation":"A — lock price for a defined window. Re-pricing at confirmation destroys customer trust and creates cart-abandonment. Standard practice in ride-hailing and delivery platforms."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No mention of regulatory constraints. Dynamic pricing / surge pricing is regulated or restricted in multiple jurisdictions, especially during declared emergencies. Without legal review, guardrails cannot be correctly defined.","affected_stakeholders":["Legal","Compliance","Finance","Customers"],"options":["A) Proceed without regulatory review and retrofit later","B) Block launch until legal review is complete","C) Launch with conservative guardrails and parallel legal review"],"recommendation":"C — launch with conservative guardrails (tight ceiling) while legal review proceeds in parallel. This limits exposure while maintaining velocity."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"Competitive positioning is mentioned but no competitive benchmarks or pricing strategy details are provided. 'Competitive' could mean cheapest, price-matching, or within a band of market rates.","affected_stakeholders":["Product","Finance","Sales"],"options":["A) Price-match competitors in real time","B) Stay within X% of market average","C) Optimize for margin with a competitiveness floor"],"recommendation":"C — optimize for margin with a competitiveness floor. Pure price-matching is a race to the bottom. The system should have a configurable competitiveness constraint."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}
